entries.intro: The deer herds near {subject_focus_the} are moving east, weeks ahead of schedule. Not a gradual drift: a full migration, all at once.
details: Usually they stay through the end of the warm season. This year they broke camp, so to speak, right in the middle of it.
They're not following their usual routes either. They're cutting through farmland, straight-line, like something's pushing them.
reactions: The hunters are scrambling. Half their season's yield just walked away overnight.
Animals know things we don't. When the whole herd agrees it's time to leave, I pay attention.

statCheck.pass: I followed the herd's trail back to where they started running. There's a stretch of dead grass in a wide band, like something passed through and killed everything it touched.
statCheck.fail: The trail merges with so many game paths that the origin point is lost. I tried following it back myself and gave up.

entries.intro: The fish in the river near {subject_focus_the} are swimming upstream, and it's not spawning season. Every species, all heading the same direction.
details: The current is strong this time of year. They're fighting it hard, packed so tight you could almost walk across their backs.
The fishermen downstream haven't caught a thing in a week. It's like someone rang a bell only the fish can hear.
Even the bottom-feeders are moving. Catfish, crayfish, everything. That never happens.
reactions: My grandfather told stories about this happening once before, when he was young. He said it meant the water downstream was dying.
It's beautiful to watch, honestly. But beautiful things that don't make sense tend to be warnings.

entries.intro: Swarms of insects have been moving through {subject_focus_the} for the past three days. Not attacking anything: just passing through in enormous clouds.
details: They're some kind of beetle I've never seen. Black shells with a green stripe. Thousands of them, maybe more.
They follow the road, which is the strangest part. They stick to the path like they know where they're going.
reactions: The children think it's fascinating. The farmers are terrified they'll eat the crops. So far they haven't touched a leaf.
I've lived here forty years and never seen anything like it. When nature does something new, it usually means something changed.

statCheck.pass: I caught a few and showed them to the old naturalist. She said they're a deep-forest species that never comes to open ground. Something drove them out of the canopy.
statCheck.fail: They move fast and do not hold still. Not much to tell beyond the obvious: there are a lot of them.

entries.intro: The wolves near {subject_focus_the} have abandoned their territory. Every pack, gone within a week. Left their dens, their cached kills, everything.
details: We found three dens with pups still in them, abandoned. The mothers just left. That goes against every instinct a wolf has.
The packs were heading northwest, into the high mountains where there's less prey, not more. It makes no sense as a survival move.
reactions: Some people are celebrating. No more wolves, no more livestock losses. I think they're fools.
The wolves kept the rabbit and rodent population in check. We're going to see the consequences in our grain stores soon enough.

statCheck.pass: I visited one of the abandoned dens. The ground around the entrance is saturated with a smell I can't place: sharp, chemical, not natural. Something contaminated their home ground.
statCheck.fail: The dens just look like holes in a hillside. Whatever drove the wolves out, it is not something I can see.

entries.intro: Birds near {subject_focus_the} have changed their nesting patterns. Species that usually spread out are all clustering together in one grove.
details: Hawks, songbirds, crows: all in the same stand of oaks. Normally they'd drive each other off, but they're tolerating the proximity.
The noise is incredible. You can hear the grove from half a mile away.
Every other tree in the area is empty. They all chose the same spot, and I'd give a lot to know why.
reactions: The children love it. They sit at the edge of the grove and watch for hours.
A traveling scholar said he'd seen this behavior once before, near a fault line, a week before the ground shook. I hope he's wrong.
